text: Star Wars is dead. That's something
you've probably heard many times over
the past few years, and it's not hard to
see why. The sequel trilogy started out
strong, only to turn into an
unmmitigated disaster. There hasn't been
a new movie in almost 6 years, and
practically every new show released on
Disney Plus has either been a critical
or commercial flop, or both. And like
anyone hurtling towards their 50th
birthday, Star Wars seems to be going
through a bit of an identity crisis
these days, desperately trying to
reinvent itself into something newer and
cooler. Which leads to the very obvious
question, what even is Star Wars now? Is
it a feminist power fantasy? A
lightweight adventure for little kids, a
dark, gritty political thriller with
sci-fi trappings, or one person's thinly
disguised sexual fetish wrapped up in
lightsabers and
spaceships. Gay. I don't really know
what Star Wars is meant to be now. But
one thing it isn't is successful. Andor,
the latest addition to the Disney Plus
lineup, cost $650 million in total and
pulled in less than 2 million viewers in
its first 3 days. Oh, come on. Jesus.
I've had videos that get more views in
the same time frame. Not only is it not
making money for Disney, but even worse
than that, Star Wars has lost every
shred of cultural relevance it once has.
People just don't talk or even care
about it anymore. And the only time they
do mention it is when some big
controversy erupts. Take Andor for
example. The only discourse of any kind
around that show is because of a
two-minute scene where an Imperial
officer tries to use the force on a
young woman. The acolyte was talked
about, but for all the wrong reasons, to
the point where Disney themselves had to
step in and confirm that never again
would they inflict another series of
that absolute catastrophe on us.
Meanwhile, Skeleton Crew, which was
pretty much free of controversies, was
also more forgettable than a cheese
sandwich and disappeared like a fart in
the winds. I actually had to look it up
to confirm that yes, it did indeed
exist. And I watched it and it cost $130
million to make. It wasn't always like
this, you know. Ask any random person on
the street who Darth Vader, Luke
Skywalker, or Han Solo is, and I'm
pretty sure they'll be able to tell you,
even if they've never even watched the
Star Wars movie before. Ask that same
person who Paul Damaran, Finn, the
Mandalorian, or Cassian Andor is, and
you'll likely be met with the kind of
vacant, deadeyed stare that would make
Billy Isish look like a model of bright,
optimistic intellects. With a string of
commercial failures like this and a
total collapse in cultural relevance,
you can't exactly blame people for
concluding that yes, Star Wars as a
brand is indeed dead. But then something
kind of strange happened. Revenge of the
Sith was re-released into cinemas to
mark the 20th anniversary of the film's
premiere, which itself is nothing
particularly unusual. Lucasfilm has a
long history of re-releasing older Star
Wars movies to mark important
anniversaries. It's a nice way to
revisit old classics. And for younger
fans, it's a chance to experience Star
Wars as it was meant to be seen on the
biggest of big screens. But this one was
different. Revenge of the Sith rad in
more than 42 million worldwide in its
first weekend, rocketing up the box
office charts like it was some
commercially viable new release instead
of a novelty trip down memory lane for
hardcore fans only. It would be like
some old heavyweight boxer coming out of
a 20-year retirement and still being
able to kick ass against top level
contenders. And let's not kid ourselves
here. This is not some cinematic classic
we're talking about. Revenge of the Sith
was the deeply flawed conclusion to a
not particularly good trilogy, and the
audience perception of it has gone
through more twists than a David Fincher
movie over the years. At first, everyone
loved it because they were hyped by the
Star Wars legacy and the pretty visuals.
But then came the YouTube video essay
era where it became fashionable to make
hour-long videos [ __ ] all over the
prequels. And now that we're trapped in
the festering swamp of the Disney era,
the rose tinted glasses are coming out
and people are starting to look back
more favorably on the prequel trilogy.
It's all relativism and it's all colored
by what happens to be going on at the
current time. But all of this is to say
that Revenge of the Sith wasn't and
still isn't a particularly great Star
Wars movie, but people flock to see it
anyway. Why? because it's the last movie
to come out that was still
unquestionably Star Wars. Flawed it
might have been, but it still embodied
the core elements of what Star Wars was
supposed to be about. The epic
adventure, the hero's journey, the
struggle between good and evil, the
family melodrama, and the love story at
its core. Simple stuff, but sometimes
the simplest ideas are also the most
timeless. On the other hand, the most
timely and current day of ideas are
likely to become the most divisive and
dated. If Disney are looking for an
answer to the ongoing question of what
Star Wars is supposed to be, well, there
it is. This right here is what people
want. For better or worse, Star Wars
apparently isn't quite as dead as we all
thought, and fans do still want more of
it. The only problem is they just don't
want your version of it. Maybe that is
something you should think about very,
very carefully. Oh, yeah, and just a
heads up that we're down to the last few
days on our Kickstarter campaign for
Trial by Fire. The response has been
absolutely amazing and we've raised over
a quarter of a million pounds already.
And now that we're on the home straight,
we're pushing hard to reach as many of
our stretch goals as possible. I know
everyone's already been super generous,
but if you're still thinking about
becoming a backer, then now is probably
the time to act. As always, the link is
in the description, and I'll leave the
rest up to
you. Anyway, that's all I've got for
today. Go away now.
